question: Q1
title: No Rate Limiting or Account Lockout on Login Endpoint
data_flow: POST /rest/user/login -> login() handler in routes/login.ts -> models.sequelize.query() with raw SQL. No middleware applies rate limiting or lockout before this handler in server.ts. Only /rest/user/reset-password and /rest/2fa/* endpoints have rateLimit() applied.
exploit_scenario: An attacker can issue unlimited login attempts against /rest/user/login with no throttling. A credential-stuffing or brute-force script can iterate common passwords against known email addresses (e.g., admin@juice-sh.op) indefinitely without triggering any block or delay.
fix_recommendation: Apply express-rate-limit middleware specifically to POST /rest/user/login with a low window (e.g., 5 requests per 15 minutes per IP). Additionally implement account lockout after N consecutive failures by recording failed attempts in the DB and temporarily setting isActive=false.

question: Q2
title: Passwords Hashed with MD5 (No Salt)
data_flow: insecurity.hash() is called from models/user.ts password setter (UserModel set password hook), from routes/changePassword.ts for current-password verification, and from routes/2fa.ts setup/disable for password confirmation. All password storage and comparison uses MD5.
exploit_scenario: MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsalted. An attacker who obtains the Users DB table (e.g., via SQL injection on the login endpoint) can crack all passwords instantly using precomputed rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated hashcat. The admin password 'admin123' would be cracked in milliseconds.
fix_recommendation: Replace crypto.createHash('md5') with bcrypt (cost factor >= 12) or argon2id. Update the hash() function and all call sites. Add a migration to re-hash passwords on next login. Ensure salts are unique per user.

question: Q3
title: RSA Private Key Hardcoded in Source Code for JWT Signing
data_flow: privateKey is a module-level constant in insecurity.ts. It is used by jwt.sign() in authorize() to sign all JWTs issued at login and during 2FA flows. The public key is read from a file (encryptionkeys/jwt.pub) and that directory is publicly browsable via /encryptionkeys route in server.ts.
exploit_scenario: The private key is embedded in source code and committed to the repository. Any developer, contractor, or attacker who reads the source (e.g., via GitHub leak or the exposed /encryptionkeys endpoint) can forge arbitrary JWTs for any user, including admin. Additionally, the /encryptionkeys directory is served publicly with directory listing enabled, leaking jwt.pub to unauthenticated users.
fix_recommendation: Remove the hardcoded private key. Load it at runtime from an environment variable or secrets manager (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit private keys to source control. Remove the public /encryptionkeys static file server route, or at minimum require authentication.

question: Q3
title: JWT 'none' Algorithm Not Explicitly Blocked; jws.verify Signature Weakness
exact_code:
export const verify = (token: string) => token ? (jws.verify as ((token: string, secret: string) => boolean))(token, publicKey) : false
export const decode = (token: string) => { return jws.decode(token)?.payload }
data_flow: verify() uses the jws library to validate the JWT signature. jws.verify() behavior with algorithm 'none' depends on library version. decode() is called after verify() in 2fa.ts: `security.verify(tmpToken) && security.decode(tmpToken)` — the decoded payload is used for authorization without re-checking algorithm type.
exploit_scenario: Older versions of jws (and jsonwebtoken < 9.0) accept tokens with alg:'none' as valid. An attacker can forge a JWT with alg:none and an arbitrary payload (e.g., role:'admin') and send it to protected endpoints. Even if jws blocks it, the code flow decodes the payload separately without verifying the algorithm field, creating a potential SSRF/confusion path.
fix_recommendation: Use jsonwebtoken's jwt.verify() with an explicit algorithms allowlist: jwt.verify(token, publicKey, { algorithms: ['RS256'] }). Never use jws.verify() which has a weaker API contract. Explicitly reject tokens where decoded header.alg !== 'RS256'.

question: Q4
title: Session Tokens Stored In-Memory Only (No Persistence, Single-Instance State)
data_flow: All authenticated sessions are stored in a plain in-memory JavaScript object (tokenMap/idMap) on the server process. There is no TTL or expiry on the in-memory session store. JWT tokens themselves expire in 6h (authorize: expiresIn: '6h'), but revocation is impossible since the tokenMap has no cleanup mechanism.
exploit_scenario: 1) A stolen JWT token that has not expired cannot be invalidated — logout does not remove it from tokenMap or blacklist it. 2) Server restart clears all sessions without warning. 3) In a clustered deployment, sessions are not shared between nodes, causing unpredictable auth failures.
fix_recommendation: Implement a proper session store (Redis or DB-backed) with TTL matching JWT expiry. Implement token revocation on logout by maintaining a blacklist or using short-lived tokens + refresh token rotation. Add periodic cleanup of expired entries from tokenMap.

question: Q4
title: Cookie Token Missing httpOnly and Secure Flags
data_flow: In updateAuthenticatedUsers() middleware, the JWT is set as a cookie via res.cookie('token', token) with no options object. No httpOnly, no Secure, no SameSite flags are specified. The cookieParser is initialized with secret 'kekse' (server.ts line 1293) but the auth cookie itself is not signed.
exploit_scenario: Without httpOnly, client-side JavaScript (e.g., via XSS) can read document.cookie to steal the auth token. Without Secure, the token can be transmitted over HTTP and intercepted via network sniffing. Without SameSite, the cookie is vulnerable to CSRF-based session hijacking.
fix_recommendation: Set cookie options: res.cookie('token', token, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict', maxAge: 6 * 60 * 60 * 1000 }). Also configure the application to enforce HTTPS.

question: Q4
title: Change Password Endpoint Accepts Parameters via URL Query String
data_flow: The change-password route is registered as a GET request, and all password parameters (current, new, repeat) are passed as URL query parameters. GET /rest/user/change-password?current=X&new=Y&repeat=Y. Passwords appear in server access logs, browser history, referrer headers, and proxy logs.
exploit_scenario: 1) Passwords in URLs are logged by morgan (combined format) in access.log files, which are publicly readable via /support/logs directory listing. 2) Referer header leaks new password to any third-party resource loaded on the page. 3) Browser history and shared bookmarks expose the new password. The access log disclosure is particularly severe — an attacker who reads /support/logs sees every password change in plaintext.
fix_recommendation: Convert to POST with password parameters in the request body (application/json). Remove the GET handler. Ensure morgan logging excludes or redacts the request body for sensitive endpoints.

question: Q5
title: Password Reset via Security Questions — Guessable Answers, No Rate Limiting
data_flow: POST /rest/user/reset-password accepts email + security answer + new password. It looks up the user's stored security answer (HMAC-SHA256), compares against submitted answer, and resets the password on match. Rate limiting is applied (100 req per 5 min window) but uses the X-Forwarded-For header as the key generator, which is attacker-controlled.
exploit_scenario: 1) Security question answers are highly guessable (mother's maiden name, pet name, etc.) — social engineering or OSINT trivially reveals them. 2) The rate limit key is extracted from X-Forwarded-For header: `headers['X-Forwarded-For'] ?? ip` (server.ts line 1350). An attacker can rotate this header to bypass the 100-request limit entirely, enabling brute force of security answers. 3) No email verification or secondary confirmation is required — password reset is instant on correct answer.
fix_recommendation: 1) Replace security questions with email-based one-time reset links (time-limited, single-use tokens stored in DB). 2) Fix rate limit key to use req.ip only (remove X-Forwarded-For trust or sanitize it). 3) If security questions are retained, implement exponential backoff and account lockout after N failed attempts.

question: Q5
title: Security Answer HMAC Uses Hardcoded Key
data_flow: Security answers are stored in the SecurityAnswers table as HMAC-SHA256 with the hardcoded key 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj'. The hmac() function is called from models/securityAnswer.ts set() hook and compared in routes/resetPassword.ts.
exploit_scenario: The HMAC key is hardcoded and committed to source. An attacker with DB access and knowledge of the key (from source code) can precompute HMAC values for common security answers offline. Unlike bcrypt, HMAC-SHA256 is fast to compute — a GPU can compute billions per second. Combined with the low entropy of security question answers, this allows rapid offline cracking.
fix_recommendation: Store security answers hashed with bcrypt or argon2 (not HMAC). Load any keying material from environment variables, never hardcode. Consider making security answers case-insensitive before hashing to reduce brute-force surface while maintaining usability.

question: Q6
title: 2FA Verification Rate Limit Too Permissive (100 req/5min) and Uses Insecure Key
exact_code:
app.post('/rest/2fa/verify',
    rateLimit({ windowMs: 5 * 60 * 1000, max: 100, validate: false }),
    twoFactorAuth.verify
  )
data_flow: POST /rest/2fa/verify accepts tmpToken (a short-lived JWT for the intermediate state) and totpToken (6-digit TOTP code). Rate limit is 100 requests per 5-minute window. The tmpToken itself expires via JWT expiry (6h from authorize()). TOTP window is set to 1 (accepts tokens 30s old or new).
exploit_scenario: A TOTP token is 6 digits (1,000,000 possibilities). At 100 requests per 5 minutes, an attacker gets 100 guesses per window. With a 30-second TOTP window and a 30-second token validity period, the attack space is limited — but 100 guesses per 5 minutes is still far too generous. The tmpToken also has a 6-hour expiry (inherited from authorize()), giving a very large window. The `validate: false` flag suppresses rate-limiter health warnings, possibly hiding misconfiguration.
fix_recommendation: Reduce rate limit to 3-5 attempts per 5 minutes for 2FA verification. Reduce tmpToken expiry to 2-3 minutes (issue a purpose-specific short-lived token). Remove `validate: false` to surface any configuration warnings. Consider invalidating the tmpToken after a single failed 2FA attempt.

question: Q6
title: 2FA TOTP Secret Stored in Plaintext in Database
data_flow: The TOTP secret is stored as a plaintext STRING in the Users.totpSecret column. It is set via userModel.totpSecret = secret in routes/2fa.ts setup(). The challenge name 'twoFactorAuthUnsafeSecretStorageChallenge' in 2fa.ts verify() confirms this is a known intentional vulnerability.
exploit_scenario: An attacker who gains read access to the Users table (e.g., via SQL injection) obtains every user's TOTP secret in plaintext. Combined with cracked MD5 passwords, this completely defeats 2FA — the attacker has both factors without any additional effort.
fix_recommendation: Encrypt TOTP secrets at rest using AES-256-GCM with a key loaded from a secrets manager (not hardcoded). Decrypt only in memory at verification time. Never log or expose totpSecret in API responses.

question: Q1
title: No Account Lockout on Password Change or 2FA Setup Endpoints
data_flow: GET /rest/user/change-password allows repeated password guesses against currentPassword with no rate limiting applied in server.ts. Similarly, POST /rest/2fa/setup verifies the user's password without rate limiting. An authenticated attacker with a valid session token (e.g., stolen session) can iterate passwords.
exploit_scenario: An authenticated attacker (e.g., with a leaked session token for a low-privilege account) can brute-force the current password via the change-password endpoint to escalate — particularly if they want to change the password and lock out the legitimate user.
fix_recommendation: Apply rate limiting to /rest/user/change-password and /rest/2fa/setup. Require re-authentication (e.g., short-lived sudo token) for sensitive account changes.

question: Q5
title: Security Question Enumeration — Email Existence Oracle
data_flow: GET /rest/user/security-question?email=X returns the security question for a registered email, and returns an empty object for unknown emails. No authentication required. No rate limiting applied on this endpoint in server.ts.
exploit_scenario: An attacker can enumerate valid email addresses by calling this endpoint: a non-empty response with a question field confirms the email is registered. This enables targeted phishing and credential-stuffing attacks against known accounts. Combined with the reset-password brute-force vulnerability, it provides a complete account takeover chain.
fix_recommendation: Return the same response structure regardless of whether the email is found (a randomly selected or fixed security question, or just a 200 OK with no data). Apply rate limiting. Require a CAPTCHA for password reset initiation.

question: Q4
title: JSONP Callback Enables Token/Email Leak via currentUser Endpoint
data_flow: GET /rest/user/whoami supports a ?callback= query parameter enabling JSONP. When callback is specified, Express's res.jsonp() wraps the response (which includes the user's id, email, lastLoginIp, profileImage) in the callback function. The challenge name emailLeakChallenge confirms this is intentional.
exploit_scenario: A malicious third-party website can include a <script src='https://juice-shop/rest/user/whoami?callback=steal'> tag. When a logged-in Juice Shop user visits the attacker's site, their browser sends the auth cookie to the Juice Shop server, and the JSONP response calls steal({user: {email: ..., id: ...}}) — leaking the user's identity to the attacker.
fix_recommendation: Remove the JSONP callback support entirely. Use CORS with explicit origin allowlists instead. If JSONP must be supported for legacy reasons, validate the callback function name against a strict alphanumeric allowlist and restrict the data returned.
